title: 2026/03/25 - EW [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-03-25 - Education and Workforce Committee - Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill [Part 2]
topic: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 25 March 2026 | Education and Workforce Committee Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill Hon Brooke van Velden, Minister for Workplace Relations and Safety Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment - Hayden Fenwick, Manager, Health and Safety Systems Policy Victoria University of Wellington - Chris Peace, Senior Lecturer in Workplace Health and Safety - Professor Joanne Crawford Engineering New Zealand - Richard Templer, Chief Executive - Joe Bain, Vice Chair, New Zealand Society for Safety Engineering; and Operations Director, Motivated Design & Analysis Engineering Consultancy Federated Mountain Clubs of New Zealand and the Aotearoa Access Climbing Trust - Allan Brent, Executive Member The Law Association of New Zealand - Brett Harris, Convenor of The Law Association's Health & Safety Law Committee St Peter's on Willis Social Justice Group - Gail Duncan, Chair - Stephen Jenkins, CPENG, Dist. FEngNZ, Member St Peter's on Willis Social Justice Group Responsible Care New Zealand (former NZ Chemical Industry Association) - Barry Dyer, Chief Executive - Ken Clarke, Technical Manager Federated Farmers of NZ Inc - David Birkett, National Board (H+S spokesperson) ShopCare - Selena Armstrong, CEO - Paul Harrison, Programme Director Stand With Pike - Rob Egan - Anna Osborne - Sonya Rockhouse Beca Limited - Rob Jury Bus and Coach Association NZ - James Kennelly, Advocacy Manager
descriptionSummary: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill. Hon Brooke van Velden, Minister for Workplace Relations and Safety. Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment

chunks.text: submissions coming through, and I'm sure you've seen them, like we all have too, that people just don't have that kara āwhina, that language. Look, I- I get... I'm sorry. Sorry to interrupt. I'm going to say, I get that there is that rationale for it, but it seems to me that the confusion is even more so at the moment.

Do you- I- Are you satisfied with that? I am satisfied. If I read it as it is listed, the hazards that are likely to result in death, like the officials have said, it's a judgment call for any business owner of what sort of action is likely to lead to death. And I guess you could raise the issue of could the word be reasonably foreseeable.

They're kind of the same thing, but slightly different definitions. So, that would be something that would be helpful for you guys to test out in the committee, but I'm not a legal drafter. But the intention there is that someone is able to make a judgment. Is an action going to lead to a death? Is it likely to lead to that death?

I'm going to stop- Is it reasonably foreseeable to lead to the death? Sorry. I've just asked if I can ask a second. Yeah, sure. It's probably of both of you. When that judgment's made, do those judgments, do you know, do they take in the sort of metric like 50% likely to occur or similar or more, over what time period? Those are the kinds of things that would be useful for us to know with that, because again, it does cause confusion.

Is there that sort of metric that sits with it? Well, there's not a specific number, just like there isn't under the current Act. And these are all things that people should be doing now. Mm. Basically what we're doing is, once you've done your assessment, narrowing down what you are actually going to be liable for as a small PCBU.

So there's still going to be, as there is now, the requirement to assess all your potential risks. Mm-hmm. And once you've done that, you should know what your critical risks are based on the likelihood of them happening. And I'm not sure whether you can ever give a percentage- Mm... likelihood of something occurring forward-looking.

You might be able to tell what has happened in the past, and that is useful information for potentially predicting what's happening in the future. And- But I'm not sure you're going to get the level of precision or accuracy. Chair's prerogative before we move on to further supplementaries. Do you see this as being any different to the current risk matrix that people currently operate in, where it is risk of it being really bad versus risk of it happening at all, and how that extrapolates out for people to determine?

No. We're not trying to fundamentally upend that. Thank you. So I've got a supplementary from Vanessa and then Pam Shear. So, there is multiple examples of how the critical risk issue is skewed already. One example is, in my electorate, Kiwi Rail wanted to put in a change of a level crossing.

They identified the risk as a critical risk of death to a pedestrian or a cyclist at that crossing of one in 1,000 years, and they deemed that to be critical, and so urgent that we must then close, do all of these things. So, it's the perverse nature of what critical risk is, and the analysis of this, actually has some really huge implications.

And this is not a small PCBU, but this whole question is one of the most vexed areas, and I'm not sure whether or not we're going to get anywhere closer with this to making it any better. It's still open for interpretation regardless. It's still massively open for interpretation. Yeah.

Can we move on to the supplementary from- Pam Shear. As most of our businesses are small businesses, ministers, and how small businesses operate are, it's different from big businesses in the terms of the kind of machinery or the equipment they have. So their machinery could be more manual than big businesses.

Mm-hmm. So having that knowledge, how it is normal for that industry to have that kind of manual equipment is really important because sometimes people will see this kind of manual, big machinery compared to something that is automated. Automated is much safer than something that is manual kind of thing. Yeah. And does this assure us that labor inspectors will have that knowledge of that industry, that this is actually normal for that manual thing?

Mm-hmm. So they are not like, because if you compare one automatic machine versus a manual machine, obviously the manual one would look like it's more kind of risky because there is more manual involvement in that. So how does this assure that the industry knowledge will be there when those assessments are done?

Look, that's a really good question. So that kind of, in some ways, sits slightly outside of the law itself. It is something that we heard a lot of on the roadshow, which is that a lot of companies, when they had machinery, And dealt with inspectors who weren't sure what they were looking at.

And that was leading to areas of confusion around the country. One clear example for me was a woman who came along to our Rosebank Road submissions, and she had two factories, one in Christchurch and one in Auckland. And the exact same machinery in both cities, was told that it was compliant in one city- Mm... told it was non-compliant in another city- Mm... because of two different WorkSafe inspectors.

No. That is some work that we have ongoing about the WorkSafe training within the regulator function, which I'm happy to come back to the committee at some point and talk about how we actually make sure that the inspectors themselves understand the law- Interesting... and understand the requirements when they are trying to follow and enforce the law.

Mm. But that won't be fixed specifically within this law itself, but more on the operations side of WorkSafe. But to the earlier point that came from our colleague Vanessa about KiwiRail. Mm. I think the difficulty here is those sorts of actions are already happening under the law that we've got at the moment.

But it ultimately is a judgment decision for every PCBU to be able to make. It's possible for WorkSafe to challenge them on how they're limiting their risks. But the important thing here is, any company can always do more- Yes. Mm, yeah... if it wants to.

There is no requirement that people must follow, like the earlier example of every specific type of action that would be listed out in the law, but some companies may simply just choose to do more. But we've been hearing that there has been overcompliance at the moment due to the lack of uncertainty in the current law.

And a good question for you to test through this process will be, does what we have drafted get any clearer for some of those companies? And Karl. Thanks, Chair. To your point, Minister, where, should this or when this passes into law, do you see WorkSafe having any role or more willingness to say to a business, "You're overcompliant here.

You're going too far. You can pull this back a bit and take some of your resources and put them into other, more productive components of your business." It would be good if that happens. It's a very good question. Look, I think the reality is that for a lot of small business owners, they will be doing that themselves, because where you have unnecessary overcompliance, it is a time and cost waster.

Mm. So for the directors of companies, as well as the managers on the ground, I expect that they'll be looking to find cost savings where they can. But the important thing is, even though they may be finding cost savings, the intention of the law is still clear that nobody should be dying, nobody should be seriously injured or ill from the work that they are doing, and they must manage those critical risks.

Mm. But we are essentially saying there is too much overcompliance, there's too much cost going into areas where it's completely wasted, and it's not actually helping keep anybody safer. It's just simply leading to more paperwork and compliance. Thank you. A good place for us to leave it.

Minister, we appreciate your time. Thank you. We look forward to hearing from our submitters and with your officials, and thank you very much- Thank you very much... for this bill. All right. Members, we will move swiftly on to Victoria University of Wellington. We have Chris Peace and Professor Joanne Crawford with us.
Together. I assume in the room.

Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you. That includes time for questions. Over to you how you use it. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you very much. My name is Professor Joanne Crawford. I work for Festival of Health and Safety, working with Victoria as- I'm Chris Peace, senior lecturer in workplace health and safety.

What we wanted to talk about this morning, and we're going to talk for about four minutes to allow you to have times for questions, is when reading the amendment bill and writing our submission, some of our major concerns. The lack of evidence that this approach is going to work. I don't know if there's evidence out there in the world or not, but I think good regulation needs to look at evidence first.

So moving forward, we need to go back and look at that. Our small PCBUs, we think there might be some confusion on this. Less fewer than 20 workers in a financial year. Is that 20 full-time employees? Is that 30, well, 19? Is that 38 part-time employees? What happens?

There's things there that we think need tightening up. And continuing. Yeah. Sorry, can I just interrupt for a moment? Are you able to bring your microphone closer to you? Yeah. Sorry. It's not just me then, Phil. Sorry. No, no. It's okay. I'm not usually this quiet. Okay. When we look at SMEs, they are the backbone of many countries.

97% in New Zealand. But international research and New Zealand research highlights that in some sectors, people are more at risk of fatality and injury, and that remains a concern. So, how are we going to support our SMEs going forward? We look at WorkSafe prosecutions.

Sorry to interrupt. And are you able to bring this right close and then potentially- Yeah. I'll try and speak up. Yeah. Sorry, I've been teaching for two days. My voice is going. No, I understand. Sorry, yeah. Okay. So WorkSafe prosecutions are mainly of small business, and there's concern that this is going to limit growth of small businesses.

Suddenly you get to, you've reached 20. Oh, we've got to suddenly start doing much more. We have concerns around managing only critical risks, because that in itself is going to potentially push all PCBUs to ignoring our chronic risks. And when we look at the breakdown of the burden of harm, our critical risks are 0.13%. Our chronic risks, our ill health, they are a much larger burden.

Mm-hmm. And it's really thinking about, when I think about the construction sector, and other sectors as an example, you have a managing company, then you have small PCBUs coming in and how is this going to come down from the managing company? I'm going to pass to my colleague Chris next.

Thank you. I haven't been teaching for two days. My concern's, in talking to colleagues, the increased complexity in the bill, and in particular in our submission you'll find there are two decision trees. One for every PCBU and one for small PCBUs. And the decision process that a thinking person in a PCBU will have to follow is increased complexity. And because of increased complexity, there may have to be greater use of other people to be advisors and to work through new systems, which increases the costs of compliance.

Joanne's already touched on it, the focus on critical risks doesn't do anything for the $5.4 billion worth of other risks that the country has to pay for every year. The non-fatal, non-critical risks that cause long-term harm to people. Because of the way the bill is structured, small PCBUs will not have to comply with a range of legislation that currently everybody is expected to comply with. As a result, best estimate, 650,000 workers in New Zealand will lose some level of statutory protection for health and safety.

A final point, we do agree with the greater use of approved codes of practice, but we are anxious that there should be greater oversight of their development by regulatory agencies, and that's essential to avoid capture by sector groups. Chairman, that's I think us- Thank you... we're open to questions from you.

Yes, we have a few questions. The first from Phil Twyford. Thanks for your submission. Would you support the retention of the critical risk concept in any form? I mean, does it have any utility? When I talk about critical risks, everyone thinks, "Oh, you're just anti them." No.

Critical risks have to be controlled in an organization, but that doesn't mean you ignore the chronic stuff. Mm. And I think that's my real concern about this bill. So, in your submission you talk about the psychosocial harms and cumulative harms. Do you think there's a place for those harms in the critical risk concept?

Yes, because if you're a large organization, psychosocial hazards may be one of your critical risks. But the same can be said for small organizations too. Mm. If I can come back in that I don't support the concept of critical risk because my analysis of WorkSafe prosecutions is it's almost certain that where people have died or suffered serious harm, they didn't believe it could happen to them. It's a psychological problem we have.

"I've been doing this job for X number of years. This has never happened to me. I've never seen it happen to anybody else, so it can't be a critical risk." That may not be true in the forestry industry, which is one of our worst performers in the country. In psychosocial, there have been instances, one case that went through the employment court process, Melville High School, where two teachers suffered severe psychosocial harm, and it resulted in one of the largest awards for damages that we've ever seen in New Zealand.

So yes, critical should include psychosocial if we're going to have such a concept as critical. Thank you. I've got a question on the point that you made about evidence. As a lecturer, have you not seen evidence for or against or otherwise? I've not done a thorough systematic search, I'll be honest.

But in terms of changing to an unbalanced system where the focus is only on critical risk, if you look at our comparators, Australia and the UK, it's a balanced approach. Mm. So you have to consider it all. Or you have to consider the hazards that your workforce are exposed to.

So. All right. And I've got a question from Francisco and then Vanessa, but it's your supplementary. We may not have time as- No. Okay. Thank you, Chair. Just a quick question around your point around 650,000 workers losing statutory protection. Is there any other law that might cover them in the space, or if they lose protection as a result of this law, they'll just lose it altogether?

I don't think so. One that really struck me was under 15-year-olds, young persons. As I read the bill, a young person will no longer be a-prohibited from working at night in the presence of machinery. If I've misread the bill, then that's telling me something about the complexity of how it's written.

So think, a young person who is working at night trying to move stuff in a business and decides that they've been told perhaps not to use a forklift truck, but they decide to use a forklift truck, and they have some event that results in serious harm to them.

One of the first incidents I ever investigated was actually a 17-year-old who was crushed underneath a forklift truck that overturned. He hadn't been trained, and it was mechanically unsound. That was something like 50 years ago that that one happened, but it's resonated for me. Another fatality involving one of a pair of twins who was killed at work because of the way the work was laid out. It was his second day at work.

And this bill as currently drafted would I think the bill will strip away. But that was a small business where that one happened. Yeah. And I think as the bill goes, if the bill proceeds in its current form, a lot of young people will find they have inadequate protection. Thank you. Okay, and we do have time, Vanessa, for a very- Good. Thank you.

With the chronic conditions, that has quite an impact on ACC. Yes. And if we were to suddenly stop trying to reduce those injuries, the potential burden- Yes... ACC, would that be your, yeah, protection. Yes. I'm coming back on Monday. Yeah.

I think yes, if we don't, we can prevent some of these chronic harms. And have we got any evidence that shows that applying health and safety rules or changes in the workplace has reduced the incidence of things like tennis elbow, wrist issues, those kind of things?

Yes. There's a body of research around musculoskeletal, work-related musculoskeletal disorders and how we can do that. Mm-hmm. And how we can use work design. Yeah. Thank you. Right. We'll now move on. Appreciate your ability- Thank you... to join us this morning and your contribution to this process.

And we'll move on next to Engineering New Zealand. We have Richard Templar, Joe Bain, and... It's just Richard and Joe. Okay, I was going to say, either Joe's got two titles or there's a mystery third person. Right. As with the previous submitter, you got 10 minutes. That includes time for questions, and it's over to you how you utilize it.

Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Kia ora. I'm Richard Templar. I'm the Chief Executive of Engineering New Zealand. With me is Joe Bain. Joe is the Operations Director of Motivated Design. Joe is a specialist in all forms of engineering safety and has worked in multiple instances dealing with the root causes of industrial accidents.

So Engineering New Zealand has 23,000 members. We are the representative body for the entire engineering profession. We regulate the profession and accredit all tertiary engineering qualifications. Our members across all sectors build, operate, and assure safety systems, provide technical leadership, and risk management advice that supports compliance with health and safety.

Our first concern is the process and focus of the bill. A lack of earlier policy work has meant that the sector has had a limited opportunity and ability to comment on the changes the bill now proposes. The breadth of the changes proposed, particularly the move to a size-based two-tier model is unexpected. We would encourage the committee to seek and publish the underlying evidence base and rationale for the proposed approach so we can better understand the policy intent. In terms of focus, health and safety legislation must remain centered on preventing harm to

workers and other persons. We're concerned that the bill weakens the consistent protections for workers and creates further uncertainties. We foresee there's a chance that the move to this bill will reverse New Zealand's recent health and safety gains. Over to Joe. Thank you, Richard.

So I'm going to speak specifically to the introduction of the two-tier system, the critical risk carve-out for PCBUs with fewer than 20 workers. And in saying this, I run a PCBU with fewer than 20 workers. Right. My life would get easier if you pass this bill. Great. I don't want you to pass this bill. It's a twist.

The primary duty of care in the current Health and Safety at Work Act requires that a PCBU and the people running it and making the decisions in it understand their business, understand the risks associated with that business, and conduct their business in a way that reduces the risk of those or that reduces the chance of those harms or those potential harms occurring.

Whether a firm has 20 workers, 18 workers, 3 workers, 30 workers, 400 workers, if they're doing the same kind of work- Mm... the risk profile is the same. Mm-hmm. Introducing a carve-out whereby small firms only have to focus on critical risks and large firms have to e-effectively stick to the same current wide-ranging, open-ended primary duty of care is actually just saying to small businesses, "You don't have to be as good at managing your business because we've carved out this special set of conditions for you."

The reduction of worker protections in small businesses is, well, there's a significant risk of reduction in worker protections, especially given the way that the- Schedule of, critical risks has been drafted, where the catchall term about things likely to cause harm is a little bit vaguely worded, and everybody's going to fall back on the scheduled list of risks.

It's not a great list. We're concerned about behavioral incentives for businesses, the tendency that there will be to game the system to keep headcount below 20 workers. Mm-hmm. And we are especially concerned about how this carve-out would apply to- Mm-hmm... the upstream duties, and this is coming out as engineers.

Many of us work in design. I run a design firm. Under this law, when we're doing design work, and we have to design it for the health and safety of people who will be affected by whatever we are designing, we would only have to design for the critical risk. And a firm with 10 more staff than us- Mm-hmm... would need to take an open-ended look at all possible risks that could accrue to people downstream from their design work, and consider all of those, and make design decisions based on those.

That's not reasonable. So we don't support the introduction of the two-tier system. Thank you, Joe. In terms of the two-tier system, it's really important that these upstream duties, if the two-tier system is adopted, are excluded, because otherwise you could have two design firms designing, say, a large structure.

One has to take into account all the possible risk of health and safety. The others would only be looking at critical risk, and that's inherently wrong. In terms of the critical risks themselves, there needs to be a much clearer definition. There is a hybrid at the moment with the lists in Schedule 1A, and then a catchall test, which uses the words, "Likely to result,"

which isn't clearly defined and is open to interpretation. We recommend there be much clearer guidance. Also in our submission, we've covered on other aspects, including the need for psychosocial risks to be included. This is particularly important to engineers, noting that in the construction sector, nine times as many people die from suicide as die from accidents.

Mm-hmm. And so that needs to be considered. We're happy to take questions on any of these points. Thank you. We have the first question from Grant McCallum, and then Pam Shepherd is next. Yeah. The small business carve-out one, I'm just interested. So the carve-outs come from the fact that some small businesses really struggle with knowing exactly what they have to do and the overwhelming requirements at times of having to deliver the same. They haven't got the expertise of a larger corporate with the professional person who just

spends their life working through this stuff. And you've got a small business, they've got two or three employees, and they're trying to make it all work, and they're the ones that I think are really struggling with some of the health and safety. They're unclear what their obligations are, and so by simplifying it, it would appear they have got that opportunity.

So I'm just curious as to why you're so against those smaller businesses actually being able to run a business much easier given the stress they're under. You want me to field that? Yeah. Yeah. So when the Health and Safety at Work Act was passed, the intention was for it to be followed up with renewed regulations, best practice guides, codes of practice, a whole pipeline of supporting information, and clarifying information, and tools for businesses, and especially small businesses to use to comply with the law.

We never did that. In the last 10 years, we just haven't had that pipeline of guidance. And I agree, that has created a situation where a lot of small businesses don't know what good looks like and don't know what they're supposed to do. The solution to that is not to amend the only part of our health and safety system that's actually reasonably modern.

It's to sort out the pipeline of guidance and regulation. And if we want to provide more guidance for small businesses about what good looks like, about how much they have to do, let's do that rather than going back to the legislation and rechanging the framework, so that now if you're below an arbitrary threshold of worker count- Mm-hmm... you have to follow a different process for thinking about the risks associated with your work.

Mm-hmm. I think the playing field should be even for all business, regardless of size. And if there are knowledge gaps to resolve, let's resolve the knowledge gaps. Right. Can I make the next questions more succinct, please, Vanessa? Sorry. Sorry, Pam Shepherd then Vanessa. So you said... No. Did you say Pam Shepherd? Sorry, it was Pam Shepherd then Vanessa. Yeah. So you said that businesses will be gaming the system to remain small businesses.

What do you mean by that? Can you elaborate on that? Because if you have a business with 17 employees- That is right... more demand for what you do, obviously you want to grow your business. Are you saying that businesses deliberately will not want to grow then? What does actually- I, I-... you mean by saying gaming the system? I think there hasn't been a system created by human beings in all of human history that hasn't been gamed by somebody.

Because- We're talking particularly about businesses wanting to grow. Yeah, because any system creates incentives. Mm-hmm. Businesses will want to grow. The question is, how will they grow? And the chances are, I think that some will choose to go, "Right, well, we've got a team of 19 in the North Island. Let's start a new company in the South Island, and we'll grow that as a separate entity,"

because that'll be easier than going over this fairly arbitrary threshold, and suddenly the compliance framework and the system we have to use to satisfy the law will change for us.It creates this- But anyway, isn't it? Yeah. Phenisa? It can happen anywhere. Yeah. Thank you. My question is around the unintended impact on upstream duties.

Mm-hmm. Is there not a professional obligation- Exactly... for engineering design- Mm-hmm... to design well, rather than it being a health and safety law implication? There is. And at the moment, there is a very neat and tidy alignment between the legal obligation on the PCBU- Yeah... and the ethical obligation on the professionals working within that firm.

Yeah. The interests are aligned, the obligations are aligned. If we change it so that the PCBU is only required to design for critical risks, but the individual engineers or architects or whatever, working within that firm have an ethical duty that goes beyond that, suddenly we have a misalignment of incentives and expectations, and in that lies risk.

Okay. Thank you very much for your time. We need to move on to our next submitter. Appreciate- Thank you... your participation in the process. Our next submitter is Alan Brent from the Federated Mountain Clubs of New Zealand. They're joining us via Zoom, so hopefully that's all working now.

Hello there. Hello, Alan. Are you able to turn your camera on? We don't have to see you, we'd just like to. Yeah. I would like to. I'm just working through some IT gremlins as per usual. Good morning. Good morning. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, Alan.

The time is yours to use as you wish, but it does include time for questions of you. Yeah. Thanks, Madam Chair. Well, good morning everyone, and first of all, thanks for having us. Yeah. My name's Alan Brent. I'm from Federated Mountain Clubs of New Zealand, which is around about a 100-year-old organization, representing 20,000 people across a broad part of the outdoor recreation community.

But today I'm speaking on behalf of 15 organizations, representing an even broader part of that community. So, people from hunting, fishing, tramping, white water, free flight, climbing. So, a really broad chunk of the outdoor community I'm speaking on behalf of today.

Just like to start by saying we're coming into this process having had a significant amount of interaction with officials and the minister, and we want to say thanks for that as well. And the reason we're so humble on it is that this is a bill about work, and we're here today bringing issues that are not about work.

We're talking about recreation. So our core underlying issue is that the Health and Safety at Work Act was designed, as so far as possible, to eliminate risks in workplaces, and we're not talking about workplaces. The regime, if you want to call it that, has crept into recreation, and it's got to the point where it's causing undue difficulty and we say that it's starting to impinge on one of the things that makes New Zealand a great place to live.

Mm-hmm. We've set out in our submission, which I take you'll have read a bit- Mm-hmm... but hopefully you've read this. We've set out five issues, but they're really in two key buckets. The first bucket has to do with concerns from landowners, that they have liability under the act towards recreationists.

We've set out a great bunch of detail and examples in our submission there. So, this issue really started winding up when WorkSafe prosecuted the landowner in the Whakaari White Island tragedy, 2019. But a good example of it, probably for lots of you folk, would be an urban one, and it's the Auckland Grammar School rock wall, the Quarry, as it's known.

So this is the birthplace of serious rock climbing in New Zealand. Very hard climbs. I've been lucky to get up a couple of them once or twice. They're that hard. It was hewn out by prisoners to make Mount Eden a prison, back in the 1880s, I think it was. And it operated for over 50 years as that, I suppose, proving ground for the entire New Zealand climbing scene.

So you think of Sir Edmund Hillary and things like that. Now, this closed shortly after the passage of the act in 2015. Okay? So this is a really good example of... We don't want to guess at the motivations of the Auckland Grammar School board, but it's a good example, I think. We've been told at least that there are perceived liability concerns.

So that's a little bit about landowner liability concerns, and we've set out a number of detailed proposals for sort of addressing that. The second bucket has to do with club liability issues. And again, these are issues of perception.So, your previous submitter, a gentleman from Engineering New Zealand, talked about a lack of guidance in his area. Over in the recreation zone, we've hit a different experience, which has been detailed guidance flopping around,

which has been really difficult for small clubs who are volunteers. They don't know what their liability is. Mm-hmm. And the latest iteration of the guidance, which I understand isn't finalized, has it that a small tramping club who employs a part-time cleaner would have full health and safety at work act duties for all of their trips and stuff into the backcountry.

Mm-hmm. So completely unrelated issues. Mm-hmm. We understand the legal logic of this, of course, but we say that the practicalities of that are wrong. So yeah, two buckets. There are a couple of other lesser issues, things like fixtures and fittings on land installed by recreationists, things like maimais.

But we'll leave it there in view of time, and happy to take any questions. Thank you very much, Alan. Can I just ask, do you see the intention of this bill and the definition between recreational and workplace, that in a workplace it's the responsibility of the PCBU, and non-workplace, it's the responsibility of the individual? Is that how you see the definition becoming as a result of this change?

Well, recreationists voluntarily take on risk on their own. Yeah. So, I mean, that's a nutshell- Yeah... if you want. Yeah. Cool. Thank you. Do we have any further questions? And can I just say, Alan, in no way is the lack of questions a lack of interest. You've got a very succinct and clear submission, so I want to thank you for that. And we can give you three minutes back in your day.

Thank you very much. No worries. Thanks for your time, and thanks for all your work. Appreciate it. Thank you. Excellent. So much. Awesome. Thanks. We'll move on now to the Law Association of New Zealand, who are joining us on Zoom. We have Brett Harris, who's the convener.

Good morning. Good morning. How are you, Brett? Good. Thank you. We do like to see your face. We don't have to, but we like to. So if you want to turn your camera on. I'm working on that. Thank you. Here we are. Hopefully you can see me now.

Wonderful. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, Brett. The time is yours to use as you wish, but it does include time for questions. Well, thank you very much. Good morning, and hopefully you have seen the submission from the Law Association of New Zealand. Yes. Was dated on the 18th of March, and most of you might recognize that name and brand from the Auckland District Law Society years ago. So I'm the convener at the moment of the Health and Safety Committee, a specialist group of about 15 lawyers that include WorkSafe representatives and

prosecutors, insurers, barristers, and specialist lawyers in this area. Myself, I've been doing this work since, excuse me, about 1999. So I've been involved in this area for quite a long time and was involved in Whakare, sadly, in the first stages, for the Buttle family.
